description: A constitutional defense education framework built on 20+ years of Canadian cannabis jurisprudence. BENO-X maps the legal argument that if the government's exemption regime (MMAR/MMPR/ACMPR) is constitutionally defective — i.e., it does not provide meaningful, practical access — then possession of cannabis outside that defective regime cannot constitute an offense.
disclaimer: Educational framework only. Not legal advice. Retain qualified legal counsel.

pillars.principle: Prohibition unconstitutional as applied to seriously ill patients lacking meaningful access
Practical access to supply is a constitutional requirement — a right without supply is no right
Restriction to dried form only was arbitrary and overbroad; patients have the right to any non-harmful form
Elimination of personal production violated s.7 by forcing patients into unaffordable commercial market
Continuing evolution: current ACMPR provisions remain subject to Charter scrutiny

argument_logic: 1. The government created a licensing/exemption regime (MMAR → MMPR → ACMPR)
2. Courts found that regime repeatedly violated s.7 Charter rights
3. A defective exemption regime = no valid exemption
4. If no valid exemption exists, possession cannot be a criminal offense
